content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing a game with two of his friends. In this game, each player chooses an integer between 111 and PPP inclusive. Let's denote the integers chosen by Chef, friend 1 and friend 2 by iii, jjj and kkk respectively; then,  Chef's score is

(((N mod i) mod j) mod k) mod N .(((N\,\mathrm{mod}\,i)\,\mathrm{mod}\,j)\,\mathrm{mod}\,k)\,\mathrm{mod}\,N\,.(((Nmodi)modj)modk)modN.

Chef wants to obtain the maximum possible score. Let's denote this maximum score by MMM. Find the number of ways to choose the triple (i,j,k)(i,j,k)(i,j,k) so that Chef's score is equal to MMM.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and P.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of ways to obtain the maximum score.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the maximum possible score is 3, which can be achieved when i, j, and k are all equal to 3 or 4. For the second test case, the maximum possible score is 2, which can be achieved when i, j, and k are all equal to 3 or 4.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to find the maximum possible score for each test case and then count the number of ways to achieve this score. The maximum possible score is N - 1, which can be achieved when i, j, and k are all equal to N. We can iterate over all possible values of i, j, and k and calculate the score for each combination. If the score is equal to the maximum possible score, we increment the count.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * P^3) — because for each test case, we are iterating over all possible values of i, j, and k.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — because we are using a constant amount of space to store the count and the maximum possible score.
